turns.content: I just got my first working tests up with the Json wrapper using serde_json::Value for our config parsing, but I'm struggling to clean up temporary files in test fixtures without writing a bunch of manual Drop implementations everywhere. It feels like every test helper needs its own cleanup logic and that's getting messy.

That sounds frustrating when you are just starting out with test infrastructure. The pattern you described is exactly what the Test Helpers and Fixtures category covers, specifically using helper structs with Drop cleanup to ensure files are removed automatically. Instead of writing manual drop implementations for every single test, you can define a reusable struct that implements Drop so it handles file removal when the scope ends. This approach lets you use parametrized tests where a single factory generates multiple scenarios without duplicating setup code.
So I should avoid creating new structs for each test and instead reuse one generic helper? That sounds cleaner than what I have right now with manual cleanup.

Exactly, that is the core benefit. By defining one struct that implements Drop to handle file removal, you can attach it as a fixture in your tests via the rstest crate. This means you don't need separate structs for every test case; instead, you create a single factory or helper that manages resources and cleans them up automatically when the test runs.
I see now how to centralize this logic so my tests stay DRY without duplicating drop implementations.

That is the right intuition. Now your test suite will be much more maintainable because you only manage cleanup in one place, and you can easily extend it with parametrized tests or additional factories as your project grows.
Hey, I'm learning Rust and doing my first tests. What's the difference between a helper struct with Drop cleanup and rstest fixtures?

Good question! When you want to manage test state or setup/teardown logic in your tests, you have two common approaches: creating a helper struct that implements `Drop` for cleanup, and using the `rstest` crate's fixture macro. The helper struct with Drop is more flexible and idiomatic for complex cases, while `rstest` provides simpler syntax for parametrized or grouped tests.
So if I just need to set up a database connection per test, would the helper struct be better?

Yes! For example, you can create a `TestEnv` struct that sets up your DB connection on creation and drops it when dropped. This way, every test gets its own clean environment without polluting each other.
Okay, but what if I want to reuse the same fixture across multiple tests? Wouldn't `rstest` be easier for that?
Absolutely! Using `rstest::fixture` macros lets you define a single test fixture with parameters, and it gets reused seamlessly. This reduces boilerplate code and makes the intent clear.
Wait, so I can't just write a helper struct that holds my DB connection and use that in multiple tests? That wouldn't be idiomatic?

Good point! However, if your test suite is small or the setup logic isn't complex, you might find `rstest` fixtures more concise. But for larger projects with lots of shared state, a helper struct with Drop cleanup is better because it gives clearer control and reusability.
So which one should I use if my tests are parametrized? Parametrize them in `rstest`?
Yes, definitely. The `rstest::fixture` macro supports parametrization directly via the `#[cfg(test)]` module, so you can write one fixture that runs with different parameters per test case.

Alright, thanks! So to sum up: if I want flexible helper structs for complex setup/teardown and reuse across tests, use a helper struct. If my tests are parametrized or grouped easily, use `rstest` fixtures.
Exactly right! You've got it. And remember, the key is clarity: if you find your code easier to understand and maintain with one approach over another, stick with that for consistency.
